# PropStealth UI Design Spec

**Date:** 2026-04-19
**Status:** Approved

---

## Visual Direction

**Style:** Clean & Professional — white backgrounds, green accents (#059669), inspired by Stripe/Notion.
**Typography:** System font stack (system-ui, -apple-system, sans-serif).
**Color palette:**
- Primary: #059669 (green — trust, money, growth)
- Background: #ffffff, #f9fafb, #f8faf8 (sidebar)
- Text: #111827 (headings), #374151 (body), #6b7280 (secondary), #9ca3af (muted)
- Attention: #fffbeb / #fde68a (amber — needs attention)
- Danger: #fef2f2 / #fecaca (red — vacancies, violations)
- Info: #eff6ff / #bfdbfe (blue — agent proposals, tenant tags)
- Purple: #7c3aed (HOA tag)

## Layout

**Navigation:** Left sidebar, always visible. Logo at top, nav items below, settings/actions at bottom separated by a divider.

**Two distinct app experiences** based on role selected at signup:
1. **Owner view** — task-centric, focused on what's happening with their properties
2. **Agent view** — client portfolio overview, tenant pipeline, help requests

## Screens

### Shared

#### Login / Sign Up
- Single page with role toggle at top: "Property Owner" | "Real Estate Agent"
- Toggle switches the messaging:
  - Owner: "Manage your rental properties with AI"
  - Agent: "Grow your clients' wealth with AI"
- Google OAuth button (primary), email/password fallback below
- "Already have an account? Sign in" link at bottom
- After signup, role determines which dashboard the user lands on

#### Homepage (Unauthenticated)
- Nav: logo, How It Works, For Agents, Pricing, Get Started CTA
- Hero: "Your rental properties, managed by AI agents" + alpha badge (Florida)
- App screenshot preview showing the dashboard UI
- Social proof stats (placeholder for alpha data)
- How it works: 3 steps with photos — Connect Gmail → Add properties → Let agents work
- Feature showcases: alternating left/right layout
  - Inbox Agent with inline mockup
  - Tenant Evaluation with inline mockup
  - Agent support section with photo
- "Built for both sides" persona cards with images
- Testimonial quote from alpha user
- Bottom CTA: "Stop managing. Start growing."
- Minimal footer: copyright, Privacy, Terms, Contact

### Owner (Dana) Screens

#### Activity Feed (Home)
- Greeting: "Good morning, Dana" with count of attention items
- "+ Evaluate Tenant" quick action button
- **Needs Your Attention** section at top:
  - Tenant proposals from agent (blue background) — links to Tenant Eval results
  - HOA violations with deadlines (amber background) — links to Inbox Agent
  - Tenant requests (amber background) — links to Inbox Agent
- **Timeline** below, grouped by day (Today, Yesterday, etc.):
  - Vertical timeline with green dots (today) and gray dots (older)
  - Each entry: title, property/context, timestamp
  - Covers inbox processing, tenant evaluations, classified emails

#### Inbox Agent
- Header: "Inbox Agent" with "Last scanned: X min ago" badge
- **Property filter tabs** across top: "All Properties" | specific property addresses
- Content organized by property, then by theme within each property
- **Theme groups** with color-coded dot + uppercase label + email count:
  - Tenant (blue #2563eb)
  - HOA (purple #7c3aed)
  - Bill (green #059669)
  - Other (gray #6b7280)
- Each email card shows:
  - Sender name, unit/property, timestamp
  - **"Key points:"** AI-generated summary (2-3 sentences)
  - **"Auto-respond"** button (green, opt-in per email) — only shown where a reply makes sense
  - **"Expand"** button — reveals full email content
- No auto-generated replies by default. User must click "Auto-respond" to trigger AI draft.
- Informational emails (bills, tax notices) do not show Auto-respond button.

#### Tenant Evaluation
Two states:

**Upload flow (New Evaluation):**
- Form: Applicant Name, Property (dropdown), Document upload (drag & drop)
- Uploaded files listed with name and size
- "Run Evaluation" button + "Cancel"
- FCRA disclosure note at bottom

**Results view:**
- Header: applicant name, property, evaluation date
- Large score badge (e.g., 82/100) with colored border (green/yellow/red)
- Recommendation badge: "Recommended — Low Risk" (green), "Review Carefully" (yellow), or "High Risk" (red)
- "AI-generated and advisory" disclaimer
- **Score Breakdown** — 4 cards in a grid:
  - Income (with rent ratio)
  - Employment (with tenure)
  - Rental History (with notes)
  - Credit Check (via TransUnion)
- **AI Summary** — narrative paragraph explaining reasoning with specific evidence
- **Evidence** — list of source documents and external reports, each clickable
- **Action buttons:** Approve Tenant (green), Decline (red), Ask Agent for Help (gray)
  - "Ask Agent for Help" bridges to the agent (Priya) — this is how Dana escalates

#### Properties (standard CRUD)
- List of properties with address, type, unit count, occupancy status
- Add/edit property form
- Per-property detail view with units, tenants, documents, key dates

#### Documents (standard CRUD)
- Per-property document vault
- Upload, auto-classify, search
- File list with type tags, upload date

#### Settings
- Gmail connection (OAuth)
- Gmail label configuration (which label the Inbox Agent monitors)
- Notification preferences
- Account settings

### Agent (Priya) Screens

#### Portfolio Overview (Home)
- "Agent Account" badge in sidebar
- Header: "Portfolio Overview — Across all clients"
- "+ Evaluate Tenant" and "+ Invite Client" buttons
- **Stats row** (4 cards):
  - Active Clients (count)
  - Properties (total across clients)
  - Occupancy Rate (percentage, green text, with X of Y occupied)
  - Vacant Units (red highlight, "needs tenants")
- **Tenant Pipeline** — 3-column funnel:
  - Evaluating → Proposed → Approved (with counts)
- **Client cards** — expandable list:
  - Each card: avatar initials, client name, property count, vacancy count
  - Vacancy badge (red) or "All occupied" badge (green)
  - "View →" link to client detail
  - Expanded: lists each property with address, bed/bath, city, occupancy status
  - Per-property badges: "Occupied", "Vacant", "1 proposed", "1 evaluating"
- **Sidebar:** Dashboard, Clients (with sub-list of client names), Tenant Pipeline (with count badge), Help Requests, Invite Client, Settings

#### Client Detail
- Drill-down from portfolio overview
- Client info, all properties, per-property status
- Link to run tenant evaluation for any vacant unit

#### Tenant Pipeline
- Full pipeline view: all tenants being evaluated, proposed, or approved
- Per-tenant: name, score, target property/client, status, propose/view buttons
- Propose action sends scored candidate to client (Dana) for review

#### Help Requests
- List of client-initiated requests
- Each shows client name, message preview, timestamp
- "View" button to see full context and respond

#### Invite Client
- Form to send invitation to a new client
- Invited client receives email, signs up as Property Owner role

## Interaction Model

### Owner ↔ AI Agents
- AI agents (Inbox + Tenant Eval) work on behalf of the owner
- Owner reviews and approves agent outputs
- No autonomous email sending — always draft-for-approval via "Auto-respond" button
- Owner sees full activity log of agent actions

### Owner ↔ Real Estate Agent
- Owner can click "Ask Agent for Help" on tenant evaluations or from any screen to escalate to their agent
- Agent sees help requests in their dashboard
- Agent can run tenant evaluations and propose candidates to the owner
- Owner receives proposals in their Activity Feed and can review/approve
- Agent does NOT have visibility into the owner's inbox activity or agent communications

### Real Estate Agent ↔ AI Agents
- In MVP, AI agents only operate for clients (owners), not for the agent directly
- Agent uses the Tenant Evaluation agent to screen candidates for clients
- Future: agents could have their own AI agents for CRM, lead management, etc.

## Key Design Decisions

1. **Role toggle at signup, not separate URLs** — same page, different messaging and post-login experience
2. **Sidebar navigation** — scales for Phase 2 agents without redesigning nav
3. **Task-centric owner home** — Activity Feed, not property grid, because with only 2 agents a property-heavy UI feels empty
4. **Opt-in auto-respond** — Inbox Agent classifies and summarizes but does NOT draft replies unless user clicks "Auto-respond"
5. **Emails grouped by theme then property** — Tenant/HOA/Bill/Other with color-coded dots, organized under property headings
6. **"Ask Agent for Help"** as the bridge — owner escalates to their real estate agent from the tenant evaluation results view
7. **Agent dashboard is portfolio health, not operations** — vacancy rates, client status, tenant pipeline. Agent is not monitoring client inbox activity.
8. **Gmail label-based scoping** — user configures Gmail filters/labels themselves; our agent watches the specified label. No managed inbox in MVP.

## Mockup Reference

Interactive mockups saved in `.superpowers/brainstorm/` (not committed):
- `visual-direction.html` — 3 style options (selected: B, Clean & Professional)
- `layout-approaches.html` — sidebar vs top nav (selected: A, Sidebar)
- `agent-dashboard-v3.html` — agent portfolio overview (approved)
- `inbox-agent.html` — inbox agent with theme grouping (approved)
- `tenant-eval.html` — upload flow + results view (approved)
- `owner-activity-feed.html` — owner home screen (approved)
- `login-page.html` — login with role toggle (approved)
- `homepage-v2.html` — unauthenticated landing page with images (approved)

1	# PropStealth Backend & Auth Design Spec
2	
3	**Date:** 2026-04-19
4	**Status:** Approved
5	
6	---
7	
8	## Overview
9	
10	Express + TypeScript backend server in a monorepo alongside the existing Next.js frontend. Handles Google OAuth 2.0 authentication, session management via JWTs in HTTP-only cookies, and serves as the API/BFF layer for the frontend. PostgreSQL via Docker for data storage.
11	
12	## Monorepo Structure
13	
14	Top-level directories. Move existing Next.js app into `web/`, create `api/` for Express. Root `package.json` uses npm workspaces.
15	
16	```
17	prop-stealth/
18	├── package.json              # workspaces: ["web", "api"]
19	├── docker-compose.yml        # PostgreSQL 16
20	├── web/                      # Next.js app (moved from root)
21	│   ├── package.json
22	│   ├── next.config.ts        # rewrites /api/* → localhost:4000
23	│   ├── middleware.ts          # JWT verification, route protection
24	│   ├── src/
25	│   └── ...
26	├── api/                      # Express backend
27	│   ├── package.json
28	│   ├── tsconfig.json
29	│   ├── src/
30	│   │   ├── index.ts          # Express app entry, listen on port 4000
31	│   │   ├── config.ts         # env vars, constants
32	│   │   ├── db/
33	│   │   │   ├── client.ts     # pg Pool singleton
34	│   │   │   ├── migrate.ts    # migration runner
35	│   │   │   └── migrations/   # SQL migration files
36	│   │   ├── routes/
37	│   │   │   └── auth.ts       # /api/auth/* routes
38	│   │   ├── middleware/
39	│   │   │   └── auth.ts       # requireAuth middleware
40	│   │   └── types.ts          # User, Session types
41	│   └── .env                  # secrets (not committed)
42	```
43	
44	## Database
45	
46	PostgreSQL 16 via Docker Compose. Two tables for MVP.
47	
48	### Docker Compose
49	
50	```yaml
51	services:
52	  postgres:
53	    image: postgres:16
54	    environment:
55	      POSTGRES_DB: propstealth
56	      POSTGRES_USER: propstealth
57	      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: propstealth_dev
58	    ports:
59	      - "5432:5432"
60	    volumes:
61	      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data
62	
63	volumes:
64	  pgdata:
65	```
66	
67	### Schema
68	
69	```sql
70	CREATE TABLE users (
71	  id            UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
72	  email         VARCHAR(255) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
73	  name          VARCHAR(255),
74	  avatar_url    TEXT,
75	  role          VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL CHECK (role IN ('owner', 'agent')),
76	  google_id     VARCHAR(255) UNIQUE,
77	  created_at    TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(),
78	  updated_at    TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
79	);
80	
81	CREATE TABLE sessions (
82	  id            UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
83	  user_id       UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
84	  expires_at    TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,
85	  created_at    TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
86	);
87	```
88	
89	- No password column — MVP is Google OAuth only
90	- Role is set at signup, immutable after creation
91	- Sessions table tracks active sessions for revocation
92	
93	## Authentication Flow
94	
95	### Google OAuth (Authorization Code Flow)
96	
97	**Sign Up / Sign In:**
98	
99	1. User selects role toggle (owner/agent) on login page, clicks "Continue with Google"
100	2. Frontend navigates to `GET /api/auth/google?role=owner` (or `agent`)
101	3. Express stores the `role` value in the OAuth `state` parameter (base64-encoded JSON with role + CSRF token)
102	4. Express redirects to Google OAuth consent screen via Passport
103	5. Google redirects back to `GET /api/auth/google/callback` with authorization code
104	6. Express exchanges code for tokens, gets user profile (email, name, avatar URL)
105	7. Express checks if user exists by `google_id`:
106	   - **New user:** Creates user row with role from state, creates session row, signs JWT, sets cookie, redirects to `/owner` or `/agent`
107	   - **Existing user:** Creates session row, signs JWT, sets cookie, redirects to dashboard for their stored role (ignores role param)
108	8. Frontend loads — dashboard layout reads role from JWT
109	
110	**Return visit:**
111	
112	1. User navigates to any `/owner/*` or `/agent/*` page
113	2. Next.js middleware reads `propstealth_session` cookie, verifies JWT signature
114	3. Valid + not expired → allow through
115	4. Expired or missing → redirect to `/login`
116	5. Wrong role for route (e.g., owner accessing `/agent/*`) → redirect to correct dashboard
117	
118	**Sign out:**
119	
120	1. Frontend calls `POST /api/auth/logout`
121	2. Express deletes session row from DB, clears cookie
122	3. Redirects to `/`
123	
124	### JWT Cookie
125	
126	- Cookie name: `propstealth_session`
127	- HTTP-only: yes
128	- Secure: yes (in production)
129	- SameSite: Lax
130	- Payload: `{ userId: string, role: "owner" | "agent", sessionId: string }`
131	- Expiry: 7 days
132	- Signed with a server-side secret (`JWT_SECRET` env var)
133	
134	### Email/Password Auth
135	
136	Not implemented in MVP. The login page shows email/password fields but they are non-functional. Google OAuth is the only working auth flow. Email/password will be added in a future iteration when password reset and email verification flows are ready.
137	
138	## API Routes
139	
140	### Auth Routes (`/api/auth/`)
141	
142	| Method | Path | Description |
143	|--------|------|-------------|
144	| GET | `/api/auth/google` | Initiates Google OAuth. Query param: `role=owner\|agent` |
145	| GET | `/api/auth/google/callback` | Google OAuth callback. Creates/finds user, sets cookie, redirects |
146	| POST | `/api/auth/logout` | Clears session and cookie |
147	| GET | `/api/auth/me` | Returns current user from JWT (userId, email, name, role, avatarUrl) |
148	
149	### Future Routes (not in this spec)
150	
151	Property CRUD, tenant evaluations, inbox agent, etc. will be added as separate specs.
152	
153	## Tech Stack
154	
155	### Express API (`api/`)
156	
157	- `express` + `@types/express` — HTTP server
158	- `passport` + `passport-google-oauth20` — Google OAuth strategy
159	- `jsonwebtoken` + `@types/jsonwebtoken` — sign/verify JWTs
160	- `cookie-parser` + `@types/cookie-parser` — parse cookies
161	- `pg` + `@types/pg` — PostgreSQL client (raw SQL, no ORM)
162	- `dotenv` — env var loading
163	- `tsx` — dev server with hot reload
164	- `helmet` — security headers
165	- `morgan` + `@types/morgan` — request logging
166	
167	### Docker
168	
169	- PostgreSQL 16 via `docker-compose.yml`
170	
171	### Next.js Changes (`web/`)
172	
173	- `next.config.ts` — add `rewrites` to proxy `/api/*` to `http://localhost:4000`
174	- `web/middleware.ts` — read JWT cookie, verify signature, protect `/owner/*` and `/agent/*` routes, redirect unauthenticated users to `/login`
175	- `web/src/app/(auth)/login/page.tsx` — "Continue with Google" becomes `<a href="/api/auth/google?role=owner">` (dynamic based on toggle)
176	
177	### Root
178	
179	- `package.json` — npm workspaces `["web", "api"]`
180	- `concurrently` — runs both `web` and `api` dev servers via `npm run dev`
181	
182	## Environment Variables (`api/.env`)
183	
184	```
185	DATABASE_URL=postgresql://propstealth:propstealth_dev@localhost:5432/propstealth
186	JWT_SECRET=<random-secret-for-dev>
187	GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID=<from-google-cloud-console>
188	GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET=<from-google-cloud-console>
189	GOOGLE_CALLBACK_URL=http://localhost:3000/api/auth/google/callback
190	PORT=4000
191	NODE_ENV=development
192	```
193	
194	Note: `GOOGLE_CALLBACK_URL` uses port 3000 (the Next.js proxy URL), not 4000, because the OAuth redirect goes through the frontend proxy.
195	
196	## Key Design Decisions
197	
198	1. **JWTs in HTTP-only cookies** — Embeds role and userId for frontend middleware to read (via a non-HTTP-only companion cookie or a `/api/auth/me` call), while keeping the token secure from JavaScript access.
199	2. **Role stored on user, not in JWT only** — JWT carries the role for convenience, but the database is the source of truth.
200	3. **No ORM** — Raw SQL via `pg` for simplicity and control. Two tables don't justify Prisma/Drizzle overhead.
201	4. **Passport.js** — Standard, well-documented Google OAuth integration. Avoids hand-rolling the OAuth flow.
202	5. **Next.js rewrites for proxy** — Frontend calls `/api/*` with relative URLs. No CORS configuration needed. In production, both can sit behind a reverse proxy.
203	6. **Google OAuth only for MVP** — Email/password fields exist in UI but are non-functional. Reduces scope significantly.
204	7. **`state` param for role** — The role selected on the login page is passed through the OAuth flow via Google's `state` parameter, so it survives the redirect round-trip.
205

# PropStealth Backend & Auth Design Spec

**Date:** 2026-04-19
**Status:** Approved

---

## Overview

Express + TypeScript backend server in a monorepo alongside the existing Next.js frontend. Handles Google OAuth 2.0 authentication, session management via JWTs in HTTP-only cookies, and serves as the API/BFF layer for the frontend. PostgreSQL via Docker for data storage.

## Monorepo Structure

Top-level directories. Move existing Next.js app into `web/`, create `api/` for Express. Root `package.json` uses npm workspaces.

```
prop-stealth/
├── package.json              # workspaces: ["web", "api"]
├── docker-compose.yml        # PostgreSQL 16
├── web/                      # Next.js app (moved from root)
│   ├── package.json
│   ├── next.config.ts        # rewrites /api/* → localhost:4000
│   ├── middleware.ts          # JWT verification, route protection
│   ├── src/
│   └── ...
├── api/                      # Express backend
│   ├── package.json
│   ├── tsconfig.json
│   ├── src/
│   │   ├── index.ts          # Express app entry, listen on port 4000
│   │   ├── config.ts         # env vars, constants
│   │   ├── db/
│   │   │   ├── client.ts     # pg Pool singleton
│   │   │   ├── migrate.ts    # migration runner
│   │   │   └── migrations/   # SQL migration files
│   │   ├── routes/
│   │   │   └── auth.ts       # /api/auth/* routes
│   │   ├── middleware/
│   │   │   └── auth.ts       # requireAuth middleware
│   │   └── types.ts          # User, Session types
│   └── .env                  # secrets (not committed)
```

## Database

PostgreSQL 16 via Docker Compose. Two tables for MVP.

### Docker Compose

```yaml
services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:16
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: propstealth
      POSTGRES_USER: propstealth
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: propstealth_dev
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data

volumes:
  pgdata:
```

### Schema

```sql
CREATE TABLE users (
  id            UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  email         VARCHAR(255) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
  name          VARCHAR(255),
  avatar_url    TEXT,
  role          VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL CHECK (role IN ('owner', 'agent')),
  google_id     VARCHAR(255) UNIQUE,
  created_at    TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(),
  updated_at    TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
);

CREATE TABLE sessions (
  id            UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  user_id       UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  expires_at    TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,
  created_at    TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
);
```

- No password column — MVP is Google OAuth only
- Role is set at signup, immutable after creation
- Sessions table tracks active sessions for revocation

## Authentication Flow

### Google OAuth (Authorization Code Flow)

**Sign Up / Sign In:**

1. User selects role toggle (owner/agent) on login page, clicks "Continue with Google"
2. Frontend navigates to `GET /api/auth/google?role=owner` (or `agent`)
3. Express stores the `role` value in the OAuth `state` parameter (base64-encoded JSON with role + CSRF token)
4. Express redirects to Google OAuth consent screen via Passport
5. Google redirects back to `GET /api/auth/google/callback` with authorization code
6. Express exchanges code for tokens, gets user profile (email, name, avatar URL)
7. Express checks if user exists by `google_id`:
   - **New user:** Creates user row with role from state, creates session row, signs JWT, sets cookie, redirects to `/owner` or `/agent`
   - **Existing user:** Creates session row, signs JWT, sets cookie, redirects to dashboard for their stored role (ignores role param)
8. Frontend loads — dashboard layout reads role from JWT

**Return visit:**

1. User navigates to any `/owner/*` or `/agent/*` page
2. Next.js middleware reads `propstealth_session` cookie, verifies JWT signature
3. Valid + not expired → allow through
4. Expired or missing → redirect to `/login`
5. Wrong role for route (e.g., owner accessing `/agent/*`) → redirect to correct dashboard

**Sign out:**

1. Frontend calls `POST /api/auth/logout`
2. Express deletes session row from DB, clears cookie
3. Redirects to `/`

### JWT Cookie

- Cookie name: `propstealth_session`
- HTTP-only: yes
- Secure: yes (in production)
- SameSite: Lax
- Payload: `{ userId: string, role: "owner" | "agent", sessionId: string }`
- Expiry: 7 days
- Signed with a server-side secret (`JWT_SECRET` env var)

### Email/Password Auth

Not implemented in MVP. The login page shows email/password fields but they are non-functional. Google OAuth is the only working auth flow. Email/password will be added in a future iteration when password reset and email verification flows are ready.

## API Routes

### Auth Routes (`/api/auth/`)

| Method | Path | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| GET | `/api/auth/google` | Initiates Google OAuth. Query param: `role=owner\|agent` |
| GET | `/api/auth/google/callback` | Google OAuth callback. Creates/finds user, sets cookie, redirects |
| POST | `/api/auth/logout` | Clears session and cookie |
| GET | `/api/auth/me` | Returns current user from JWT (userId, email, name, role, avatarUrl) |

### Future Routes (not in this spec)

Property CRUD, tenant evaluations, inbox agent, etc. will be added as separate specs.

## Tech Stack

### Express API (`api/`)

- `express` + `@types/express` — HTTP server
- `passport` + `passport-google-oauth20` — Google OAuth strategy
- `jsonwebtoken` + `@types/jsonwebtoken` — sign/verify JWTs
- `cookie-parser` + `@types/cookie-parser` — parse cookies
- `pg` + `@types/pg` — PostgreSQL client (raw SQL, no ORM)
- `dotenv` — env var loading
- `tsx` — dev server with hot reload
- `helmet` — security headers
- `morgan` + `@types/morgan` — request logging

### Docker

- PostgreSQL 16 via `docker-compose.yml`

### Next.js Changes (`web/`)

- `next.config.ts` — add `rewrites` to proxy `/api/*` to `http://localhost:4000`
- `web/middleware.ts` — read JWT cookie, verify signature, protect `/owner/*` and `/agent/*` routes, redirect unauthenticated users to `/login`
- `web/src/app/(auth)/login/page.tsx` — "Continue with Google" becomes `<a href="/api/auth/google?role=owner">` (dynamic based on toggle)

### Root

- `package.json` — npm workspaces `["web", "api"]`
- `concurrently` — runs both `web` and `api` dev servers via `npm run dev`

## Environment Variables (`api/.env`)

```
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://propstealth:propstealth_dev@localhost:5432/propstealth
JWT_SECRET=<random-secret-for-dev>
GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID=<from-google-cloud-console>
GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET=<from-google-cloud-console>
GOOGLE_CALLBACK_URL=http://localhost:3000/api/auth/google/callback
PORT=4000
NODE_ENV=development
```

Note: `GOOGLE_CALLBACK_URL` uses port 3000 (the Next.js proxy URL), not 4000, because the OAuth redirect goes through the frontend proxy.

## Key Design Decisions

1. **JWTs in HTTP-only cookies** — Embeds role and userId for frontend middleware to read (via a non-HTTP-only companion cookie or a `/api/auth/me` call), while keeping the token secure from JavaScript access.
2. **Role stored on user, not in JWT only** — JWT carries the role for convenience, but the database is the source of truth.
3. **No ORM** — Raw SQL via `pg` for simplicity and control. Two tables don't justify Prisma/Drizzle overhead.
4. **Passport.js** — Standard, well-documented Google OAuth integration. Avoids hand-rolling the OAuth flow.
5. **Next.js rewrites for proxy** — Frontend calls `/api/*` with relative URLs. No CORS configuration needed. In production, both can sit behind a reverse proxy.
6. **Google OAuth only for MVP** — Email/password fields exist in UI but are non-functional. Reduces scope significantly.
7. **`state` param for role** — The role selected on the login page is passed through the OAuth flow via Google's `state` parameter, so it survives the redirect round-trip.

# PropStealth — Progress & Next Steps

**Updated:** 2026-04-25
**Branches:** `main` (merged through PR #1), `feat/backend-auth` (PR #2 open), `feat/exe-dev-testing` (active)

---

## Completed

### Phase 0: PRD & Design (merged to main)
- PRD v0.2 — focused on real estate agents + small landlords (1-5 properties), 2-agent MVP (Inbox Agent + Tenant Evaluation)
- UI Design Spec — all screens for both Owner (Dana) and Agent (Priya) roles, approved with interactive mockups

### Phase 1: Static UI (merged to main via PR #1)
- Next.js 16 + Tailwind v4 + TypeScript project
- 15 static pages with hardcoded mock data, no backend
- Pages: Homepage, Login/Signup, Owner (Activity Feed, Inbox Agent, Tenant Eval list + results, Properties list + detail, Documents, Settings), Agent (Portfolio Overview, Client Detail, Tenant Pipeline, Help Requests, Invite Client)

### Phase 2: Backend & Auth (PR #2, branch `feat/backend-auth`)
- Monorepo restructure: `web/` (Next.js) + `api/` (Express + TypeScript)
- Docker Compose with PostgreSQL 16
- Database schema: `users` and `sessions` tables with migration runner
- Google OAuth via Passport.js with JWT sessions in HTTP-only cookies
- Next.js middleware for route protection (redirects unauthenticated users)
- Next.js rewrites to proxy `/api/*` to Express on port 4000
- Login page wired to real Google OAuth flow (email/password disabled for MVP)
- API routes: `GET /api/auth/google`, `GET /api/auth/google/callback`, `POST /api/auth/logout`, `GET /api/auth/me`, `GET /api/health`

### Testing Sandbox (branch `feat/exe-dev-testing`)
- exe.dev sandbox provisioned at `wyvern-zebra.exe.xyz`
- Node.js 22, PostgreSQL 16 installed on sandbox
- Database created and migrations run
- Google OAuth credentials configured (callback: `https://wyvern-zebra.exe.xyz/api/auth/google/callback`)
- Full sign-in flow verified and working
- Deploy/status/stop scripts in `scripts/`
- Web runs on port 8000, API on port 4000
- Accessible at `https://wyvern-zebra.exe.xyz`

## Next Steps

### Immediate
1. **Merge PR #2** — backend auth branch to main
2. **Merge exe.dev testing branch** — sandbox scripts to main

### Short-term (MVP functionality)
3. **User profile in dashboard** — show logged-in user's name/avatar in sidebar, replace hardcoded "Dana" / "Priya" with real user data from `/api/auth/me`
4. **Property CRUD API** — REST endpoints for creating, reading, updating, deleting properties. Wire owner Properties/Documents pages to real data instead of mock data.
5. **Tenant Evaluation API** — file upload endpoint, store evaluations in PostgreSQL, wire Tenant Eval pages to real data
6. **Agent-client relationship** — invite flow, client association, agent can see client properties

### Medium-term (AI Agents)
7. **Agent framework** — common agent interface, tool registry, structured traces
8. **Inbox Agent** — Gmail API OAuth integration, email classification, key points extraction, auto-respond drafts
9. **Tenant Evaluation Agent** — document analysis (OCR/parsing), credit check partner integration, risk scoring with AI narrative

### Later
10. **Email/password auth** — registration, login, password reset, email verification
11. **Deployment** — Vercel (web) + cloud hosting (api), production PostgreSQL
12. **Phase 2 agents** — Maintenance Coordinator, Portfolio Analyst, Bills & Utilities

content.text:
Here's what I have so far. The new shape is essentially: **agent ↔ customer ↔ tenant-review** with explicit sharing, doc upload, AI scoring, and per-tenant Q&A. Most existing infrastructure stays useful (Google OAuth, users table, properties); the Inbox/Gmail agent and the broader "system of action" framing get archived.

Before I draft the design, I want to ask a few questions one at a time. First and most architecturally important:

**Question 1: Customer Q&A — what shape is the back-and-forth?**

When a customer reviews a shared tenant and "asks a question," and the agent replies — what's the structure?

- **A. Per-tenant comment thread** — one chronological thread of messages attached to each tenant. Customer asks, agent replies, threaded by time. Simple, like a single chat per tenant.
- **B. Per-document/per-section anchored questions** — customer can pin a question to a specific document or to a section of the AI summary ("about the credit report — why is the score so low?"). More structured, more work to build.
- **C. Per-tenant thread, plus a top-level "decision" event** — same as A, but approve/reject is a distinct first-class event in the timeline (not just a button outside the thread). Lets the conversation include "I'm leaning approve, but..." reasoning.

I'd lean **A or C** — start with one chronological thread per tenant. C adds very little cost over A and makes the timeline more useful. B is overkill for v1.

Which feels right to you?

hookInfos.command: sh -c 'if ! command -v entire >/dev/null 2>&1; then exit 0; fi; exec entire hooks claude-code stop'
bash "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/hooks/stop-hook.sh"

lastPrompt: Ok we are going to take a different course. We will need a new branch, and we're going to make this a much simpler experience first. There are two actors: The real estate agent, and the Customer. The…

aiTitle: Redesign tenant screening as simplified two-actor system

message.content: A

content.text:
Got it — per-tenant comment thread, A.

**Question 2: How does a tenant relate to a property?**

When the agent creates a tenant record, do they pick a specific property?

- **A. Tenant is always tied to one specific property** — agent must choose a property when creating the tenant. The customer reviews "candidates for 123 Main St."
- **B. Tenant is just attached to the customer; property is optional** — agent can mark "this is for 123 Main St" or leave it blank. Useful if the customer is still deciding which property to fill, or has only one.
- **C. Tenant has no property field at all** — properties are just informational records. Tenants stand alone for the customer.

A is cleanest if the customer always has multiple properties; B is more flexible for early-stage customers; C is simplest but loses useful context (you can't filter "tenants for unit 2B").

I'd lean **B** — required-feeling but not enforced, so single-property customers don't have to fake-pick. Fine?

hookInfos.command: sh -c 'if ! command -v entire >/dev/null 2>&1; then exit 0; fi; exec entire hooks claude-code stop'
bash "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/hooks/stop-hook.sh"

message.content: Actually for Question 1, let's do C. Question 2: A

content.text:
Updated: Q1 = C (thread + first-class decision events), Q2 = A (tenant always tied to a property).

**Question 3: The "special link" in the customer's email — how does access work?**

When the customer clicks the link in the share notification:

- **A. Magic link, no login required** — link contains a signed token; clicking it logs them in directly (or grants access for that share). Lowest friction. Risk: forwarded emails grant access; tokens need expiry/revocation.
- **B. Link goes to a login page; customer signs in with Google first, then lands on the tenant** — more friction on first share, but their identity is verified every visit. Requires the customer to have accepted the agent's invite already (so a user record exists for their email).
- **C. Hybrid: first share is a magic-link invite (creates the account on click + Google auth), every share after is a regular notification with a deep link that requires being logged in** — first-touch is frictionless; ongoing reviews require auth.

You said earlier "Both the agent and the [customer] should use Google to authenticate." That rules out pure A. **C** matches that intent best — the invite email is the magic moment, and after that the customer is a real Google-authenticated user.

Sound right, or do you want plain B (every share requires already-being-logged-in)?

hookInfos.command: sh -c 'if ! command -v entire >/dev/null 2>&1; then exit 0; fi; exec entire hooks claude-code stop'
bash "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/hooks/stop-hook.sh"

message.content: C

content.text:
**Question 4: What documents does the agent upload per tenant?**

This drives the schema, the AI prompt, and the upload UI. Three levels of structure:

- **A. Free-form** — single "documents" upload zone, any file types (PDF/image), agent can label each one with a free-text name. AI looks at all of them together. Simplest UI, most flexible, AI has to figure out what each doc is.
- **B. Light categorization** — upload zone with a small fixed set of categories the agent picks per file: *Application*, *ID*, *Income (pay stubs / offer letter)*, *Credit/Background*, *References*, *Other*. AI gets typed inputs, can score each category. Slightly more structure, much better AI output.
- **C. Structured form + docs** — agent fills in a tenant form (name, contact, employer, income, prior address, etc.) AND uploads supporting docs. AI verifies the form against the docs. Most work, most powerful output.

I'd lean **B** — it's the right balance for a v1: minimal data entry burden on the agent, but enough structure that the AI can produce a meaningful per-category score (income / credit / history) instead of one fuzzy number.

Pick one, or describe what you actually have in mind.

hookInfos.command: sh -c 'if ! command -v entire >/dev/null 2>&1; then exit 0; fi; exec entire hooks claude-code stop'
bash "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/hooks/stop-hook.sh"

message.content: B, but the AI should attempt to auto populate the tenant details (basic details only) from the docs. The real estate agent can then correct those details in the structured form.

content.text:
Good — B with AI extraction. So the upload flow is: agent uploads docs (categorized) → AI extracts basic tenant fields → agent reviews/corrects the structured form → AI generates the full evaluation.

**Question 5: What does the AI evaluation actually output?**

What does the customer see when they review a shared tenant?

- **A. Single score (0–100) + recommendation badge (green/yellow/red) + narrative summary + cited evidence** — like the existing tenant-eval design. Simple, single number to decide on.
- **B. Per-category sub-scores (Income, Credit, History, Identity) + overall score + narrative + cited evidence** — matches the categorized upload from Q4; customer sees where strengths/concerns are.
- **C. Same as B, plus an explicit "concerns/red flags" list and a "verified facts" list** — adds two surfaces the customer can scan: things to worry about, things confirmed by docs. Heavier prompt, more useful UI.

Compliance constraint to flag: per the original PRD, FCRA + Fair Housing rules apply — the AI must not reference protected-class attributes, every claim should cite a source doc, and the output must be marked advisory. That carries forward regardless of A/B/C.

I'd lean **C**. It costs almost nothing extra in the prompt and the "concerns" list is what the customer is actually looking for when they decide to ask a question. Fine?

hookInfos.command: sh -c 'if ! command -v entire >/dev/null 2>&1; then exit 0; fi; exec entire hooks claude-code stop'
bash "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/hooks/stop-hook.sh"

message.content: C

content.text:
**Question 6: Email notifications — when does each party get notified?**

Email is the off-platform glue. Which of these events trigger an email?

| Event | Notify customer | Notify agent |
|---|---|---|
| Agent invites customer (first time) | ✓ (magic-link invite) | — |
| Agent shares a tenant with the customer | ✓ | — |
| Agent posts a reply in the tenant thread | ✓ | — |
| Customer asks a question in the tenant thread | — | ✓ |
| Customer approves or rejects the tenant | — | ✓ |
| AI evaluation finishes (after upload) | — | ✓ (optional) |

Two questions on top of that:

1. Should we **batch** customer notifications (e.g., one digest per day if the agent shares 3 tenants at once)? Or always one email per event? **One-per-event** is simpler for v1 and matches the "high-signal, low-volume" expectation here. Recommend that.
2. What email provider — **Resend** (modern, easy templates, good DX) or stick with whatever the existing repo has? Repo has no transactional email yet, so this is a green-field choice. Recommend **Resend**.

The table above is what I'd ship. Want to add/remove any rows, or change anything?

content.text:
Local SMTP via nodemailer makes sense — env-configurable host/port, can point at Mailtrap or maildev in dev, real SMTP in prod.

**Two final questions, then I'll draft the design.**

**Question 7: Document storage location.**

Uploaded tenant docs need to live somewhere.

- **A. Local filesystem** (`api/uploads/<tenantId>/<filename>`) — simplest, no external dep, works in dev and on the exe.dev sandbox. Files travel with the API server.
- **B. S3-compatible object storage** (AWS S3, MinIO, R2) — proper for prod, but introduces a service dependency and another set of credentials.

Given you said "no commercial services right now," I'd go with **A** for v1, with the upload path abstracted behind a simple `storage.put(key, file) / storage.get(key)` interface so swapping to S3 later is a small change. OK?

**Question 8: Tenant lifecycle / decision finality.**

The status flow I have in mind:

```
Draft  →  Evaluating  →  Ready  →  Shared  →  Approved | Rejected
                                       ↑
                                    (customer can ask questions
                                     while in Shared state)
```

- **Draft** — agent has created the tenant + property pick, may still be uploading docs
- **Evaluating** — AI is running extraction + scoring
- **Ready** — AI done, agent reviewing; not yet visible to customer
- **Shared** — visible to customer; thread open; customer can approve/reject
- **Approved / Rejected** — terminal; thread can stay open for follow-up but the decision is locked

Two sub-questions:
1. After Approved/Rejected, can the customer **change their mind** (re-open)? I'd say **no** for v1 — terminal means terminal. Agent can always create a new tenant record if needed.
2. Can the agent **un-share** (revoke a share)? I'd say **no** for v1 — once shared, it's shared. Keeps things simple.

Both "no" sound right?

content.text:
Great — I have enough now. Before I draft sections of the design, one architectural choice on the migration:

**Migration approaches — how do we get from the current code to the new shape?**

- **A. Fresh start.** New branch, fresh codebase, copy only what's reused (auth, user table, OAuth wiring, basic property model). Faster to reason about; loses commit history continuity for components we keep; risks re-doing fiddly OAuth bits.
- **B. Surgical migration (recommended).** Same branch lineage. *Remove* what's gone: Inbox/Gmail agent (`api/src/agents/`, `api/src/routes/inbox.ts`, `api/src/routes/gmail.ts`, `web/src/app/(dashboard)/owner/inbox`, `web/src/app/(dashboard)/owner/documents`, settings Gmail block, help-requests, activity-feed). *Restructure* what changes: rename `owner` → `customer` semantics, replace tenant-eval flow, replace agent dashboard. *Keep as-is*: Google OAuth, users + sessions tables, agent-client invite flow (already exists in `clients.ts` + 004 migration), basic properties model + migration, sandbox scripts, Next.js + Express plumbing, JWT cookie auth.
- **C. Parallel build.** Keep old routes alive under `/legacy/*` while new ones go to `/customer/*` and `/agent/*`. Heavier; only worth it if we needed both running. We don't.

I recommend **B**. Most of the auth/infra work pays forward; only the agent + email-feature surface needs to come out. Sound good?

If yes, I'll start presenting the design in sections (data model first, then screens, then flows, then code-change inventory). Scale of each will match its complexity.

content.text:
Now I'll present the design in sections, getting your approval on each.

---

## Section 1: Data Model

Renaming `owner` → `customer` throughout (role + variable names + URL paths) to match your terminology.

**Existing tables we keep, with light edits:**

- **`users`** — same as today, but `role` enum changes from `('owner','agent')` to `('agent','customer')`. We'll write a migration that renames existing values.
- **`sessions`** — unchanged.
- **`agent_customers`** (currently `agent_clients`) — table name + FK column names renamed. Add `invite_token VARCHAR(64)`, `invite_token_expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ` for the magic-link first-share invite (Q3=C).

**Existing `properties` — simplified:** drop fields we don't need for this scope (mortgage holder, insurance carrier, HOA, tax parcel, purchase price, current value). Keep: `id`, `customer_id`, `created_by_agent_id`, `address`, `city`, `state`, `zip`, `property_type`, `bedrooms`, `bathrooms`, `monthly_rent_target`, `notes`, timestamps. Customer can edit; agent can edit; nobody else.

**New tables (replacing `evaluations` and `agent_runs`/`gmail_connections`/`inbox_emails`):**

- **`tenants`** — one row per prospective tenant.
  Fields: `id`, `property_id` (required, FK), `created_by_agent_id`, `status` (`draft|evaluating|ready|shared|approved|rejected`), `shared_at`, `decided_at`, `decision_by_user_id`, `decision_note`, plus the AI-extracted-then-confirmed basic fields: `applicant_name`, `email`, `phone`, `employer`, `monthly_income`, `move_in_date`, `notes`, timestamps.

- **`tenant_documents`** — one row per uploaded file.
  Fields: `id`, `tenant_id`, `category` (`application|id|income|credit|reference|other`), `filename`, `storage_key` (relative path under `api/uploads/`), `mime_type`, `size_bytes`, `uploaded_by_user_id`, `uploaded_at`.

- **`tenant_evaluations`** — one row per AI run (re-running creates a new row; latest is current).
  Fields: `id`, `tenant_id`, `status` (`running|complete|failed`), `overall_score` (0–100), `recommendation` (`low_risk|review|high_risk`), `category_scores` JSONB (income/credit/history/identity, each `{score, summary}`), `summary` TEXT, `concerns` JSONB `[{text, source_document_id}]`, `verified_facts` JSONB `[{text, source_document_id}]`, `model_used`, `error`, `created_at`, `completed_at`.

- **`tenant_thread_events`** — unified timeline per tenant (Q1=C).
  Fields: `id`, `tenant_id`, `type` (`message|shared|unshared|approved|rejected|reopened`), `author_user_id`, `body` TEXT (for messages), `created_at`. The thread renders as a chronological mix of messages and decision events.

- **`email_outbox`** — queued/sent notifications.
  Fields: `id`, `to_email`, `to_user_id` (nullable, for invites to non-users), `subject`, `body_html`, `body_text`, `template_key`, `status` (`pending|sent|failed`), `attempts`, `last_attempt_at`, `sent_at`, `error`, `created_at`. Worker polls and sends via nodemailer.

**Tables we delete (migrations stay in history; new migration drops them):**
`gmail_connections`, `inbox_emails`, `agent_runs`, `evaluations` (replaced by `tenants` + `tenant_evaluations`).

Does the data model look right? Anything to add, remove, or rename?

content.text:
Noted — keeping `owner` everywhere (table fields, routes, role enum, existing `agent_clients` table). Section 1 stands as-is with `customer_id` → `owner_id`.

---

## Section 2: User & Auth Flows

**Sign-up paths:**

- **Agent** signs up directly: visits `/login`, picks role "Real Estate Agent," does Google OAuth. New user row with `role='agent'`.
- **Owner** is invite-only in v1: there's no role toggle for owners on `/login`. They land via the invite email.

**Agent invites an owner (first-share invite — Q3=C):**

1. Agent fills invite form: owner email + name (existing `/agent/invite` page, mostly reusable).
2. Backend creates pending `agent_clients` row tied to that email, generates a single-use `invite_token` (64-char URL-safe random, 14-day expiry), enqueues an email with link `https://app/.../invite/<token>`.
3. Owner clicks link → backend looks up token → if expired or used, show "Ask your agent to resend." If valid:
   - Page shows "Your agent <X> invited you. Continue with Google to accept."
   - "Continue with Google" goes through the standard OAuth flow with the token in `state`.
   - Callback: creates user with `role='owner'`, marks `invite_token` consumed, links the `agent_clients` row to the new user, sets session cookie, redirects to `/owner` (or directly to the first shared tenant if one exists).

**Subsequent shares:**

When the agent shares a tenant with an already-onboarded owner: backend writes the `tenant_thread_events` row (`type='shared'`), enqueues a notification email containing a deep link `https://app/.../owner/tenants/<id>`. Click → if not logged in, middleware redirects to `/login` with `next=...` → after Google login → redirect to the tenant page.

**Multi-tenant batched share:** the agent's share UI lets them select 1–N "ready" tenants and share them in one action. Backend creates one `tenant_thread_events` row per tenant (each with `type='shared'`), but enqueues a *single* email per recipient owner that lists all the tenants shared in that action. Subject: "<Agent name> shared 3 tenant candidates with you."

**Login page changes:**
- Remove the role toggle. The page is for agents only ("Sign in to PropStealth"). One Google button.
- Owners never see `/login` directly — they always arrive via an invite link or a deep-link redirect after they've been onboarded.

**Session behavior:** unchanged from current — JWT in HTTP-only cookie, 7-day expiry, Next.js middleware verifies on `/agent/*` and `/owner/*`.

**Route protection:** middleware also checks role: an `owner`-role user hitting `/agent/*` gets redirected to `/owner`, and vice versa.

Does the auth flow look right? Two specific points to confirm:
1. Login page is agent-only (no role toggle); owners are invite-only.
2. Invite tokens are 14-day, single-use — OK or different expiry?

Updated: login page keeps the agent/owner toggle; invite is the *first-time* onboarding path, but onboarded owners can return to `/login` and sign in directly.

---

## Section 3: Screens

**Marketing / Auth (unchanged or trivial edits):**
- `/` — homepage. Trim copy to match the simpler product (drop "Inbox Agent," "system of action" — replace with "Tenant evaluation, shared with your client"). One pass for content; layout untouched.
- `/login` — agent/owner toggle stays. Email/password fields stay disabled.
- `/invite/<token>` (new) — invite landing page. Shows agent name, "Continue with Google" button, fallback messaging for expired/used tokens.

**Agent screens (`/agent/*`):**

- `/agent` — **Dashboard**. List of owner-clients (from `agent_clients`). Each row: owner name, count of properties, count of tenants by status (e.g., "2 awaiting review, 1 approved this week"). "+ Invite Owner" CTA. Replaces the existing portfolio-overview's stats blocks; the card list stays similar but counts come from real data.
- `/agent/owners/[id]` — **Owner detail**. Shows that owner's properties (with a quick "+ Add Property" inline) and a flat list of tenants for that owner across all their properties. Filters: by property, by status. Replaces existing `/agent/clients/[id]`.
- `/agent/owners/[id]/properties/new` and `/agent/owners/[id]/properties/[propId]` — property create/edit forms.
- `/agent/tenants/new` — **Create tenant** flow:
  1. Pick owner + property (defaults if entered from owner page).
  2. Upload docs zone with category dropdown per file (Application, ID, Income, Credit, Reference, Other).
  3. "Run AI extraction" button → status spinner → form pre-populated with extracted basics (`applicant_name`, `email`, `phone`, `employer`, `monthly_income`, `move_in_date`).
  4. Agent reviews/corrects form, clicks "Run Evaluation" → status moves to `evaluating` → polls/streams until `ready`.
  5. Agent reviews evaluation; "Share with owner" or "Save draft."
- `/agent/tenants/[id]` — **Tenant detail (agent view)**. Three areas:
  - Header: applicant name, property address, status pill, decision pill if any.
  - Tabs: **Documents** (list, click to view), **Evaluation** (current AI scores/summary/concerns/verified facts/cited evidence), **Thread** (chronological timeline of messages + decision events; agent has a reply box).
  - Action bar: Share / Unshare / Re-run evaluation / Edit basics.
- `/agent/invite` — **Invite Owner** (mostly existing UI). Form: email, name, optional first message. Existing pending-invite list stays.
- `/agent/settings` — minimal: name, avatar, sign out. Drop Gmail block.

**Owner screens (`/owner/*`):**

- `/owner` — **Dashboard**. Two sections:
  - "Tenants to review" — cards for `status='shared'` tenants, with score, recommendation, property address.
  - "Recent activity" — last 10 thread events across all their tenants (new messages from agent, etc.).
  - List of properties below, each with a "n tenants" link.
- `/owner/properties` — list of properties. Click into one to view/edit details.
- `/owner/properties/[id]` — property detail; owner can edit basics (the same fields the agent set up).
- `/owner/tenants` — full tenants list, filterable by property and status.
- `/owner/tenants/[id]` — **Tenant detail (owner view)**. Same three-area layout as agent view, but:
  - No "Share/Unshare/Re-run" actions.
  - Action bar: Approve / Reject / Reopen (if already decided).
  - Reply box on the thread for asking questions.
- `/owner/settings` — name, avatar, notification preferences (one toggle for now: "email me about tenant activity"), sign out. Drop Gmail block.

**Removed pages (delete the files):**
- `/owner/inbox` (entire route)
- `/owner/documents` (entire route — out of scope; tenant docs live on the tenant)
- `/owner/tenant-eval/*` — replaced by `/owner/tenants/*`
- `/agent/clients/*` — renamed to `/agent/owners/*`
- `/agent/pipeline` — Tenant Pipeline; the per-owner detail page covers this; we can revisit later.
- `/agent/help-requests` — replaced by per-tenant threads.

Does the screen inventory look right? In particular:
1. Replacing "Pipeline" + "Help Requests" with the per-tenant thread on the tenant page — OK, or do you want a global "Threads with new messages" page for the agent?
2. Owner dashboard's "Recent activity" section — useful, or skip for v1?

content.text:
---

## Section 4: AI Evaluation Pipeline

**Two AI calls per tenant**, both via `@anthropic-ai/sdk` with Claude (already in `api/package.json`). Use prompt caching on the system prompt + doc text since both calls use overlapping context.

**Step 1 — Extraction (after upload, before agent confirms basics):**

- Input: all uploaded docs converted to text. PDFs via `pdf-parse`; images via Claude's vision input directly (Claude can read images natively, so we skip an OCR dep).
- Prompt: "Extract the applicant's basic info from these documents. Return JSON with applicant_name, email, phone, employer, monthly_income, move_in_date. Use null for any field you can't confidently determine."
- Output: structured JSON. Stored on `tenants` row but flagged "ai_extracted=true" until agent confirms (or just trust the agent will edit). The form pre-populates from this; agent corrects and saves.

**Step 2 — Evaluation (after agent clicks "Run Evaluation"):**

- Input: agent-confirmed basic fields + all docs (text + images) + property context (rent target, address). Each doc gets a stable `document_id` the model is told about so it can cite.
- Prompt: produces a single JSON output matching the `tenant_evaluations` schema:
  - `overall_score` (0–100)
  - `recommendation` (`low_risk|review|high_risk`)
  - `category_scores`: `{ income: {score, summary}, credit: {score, summary}, history: {score, summary}, identity: {score, summary} }`
  - `summary`: narrative paragraph
  - `concerns`: array of `{text, source_document_id}`
  - `verified_facts`: array of `{text, source_document_id}`
- **Compliance guardrails baked into the system prompt** (FCRA + Fair Housing): never reference protected-class attributes (race, religion, national origin, sex, familial status, disability, age, source of income except as legally permitted), all claims must cite a source doc, output is advisory only. Drop the guardrails as a constant in `api/src/agents/tenant-eval/prompts.ts` so they're auditable.

**Execution model:**

- Both steps run **inline in the request handler** for v1 (no job queue). Extraction is fast (~5s); evaluation is ~10–20s. If we outgrow this, we move to a worker process — but adding a queue now is YAGNI.
- The endpoint that triggers eval returns immediately with the new `tenant_evaluations` row in `status='running'`; the model call runs in a fire-and-forget async function that updates the row when done. Frontend polls `/api/tenants/<id>` every 2s while status is `evaluating`.
- On error: `tenant_evaluations.status='failed'` with `error` populated; UI shows "Evaluation failed — try again" and the agent can re-run.

**Re-runs:** Each "Run Evaluation" creates a new `tenant_evaluations` row. The "current" evaluation displayed is the most recent `complete` one for that tenant. Old evaluations stay for audit.

**Storage of doc text:** we don't pre-extract and store text — every eval reads the files fresh from disk. Simpler, fine at this scale. If files get large, revisit.

**Code location:** `api/src/agents/tenant-eval/{index.ts, extract.ts, evaluate.ts, prompts.ts}`. Replaces `api/src/agents/inbox/*` after deletion.

Does the pipeline look right? Two specifics:
1. Inline async (no queue) for v1 — OK, or do you want a proper job worker now?
2. The four eval categories — Income / Credit / History / Identity. Add or change?

content.text:
---

## Section 5: Notifications & Email Worker

**Provider:** [`nodemailer`](https://nodemailer.com) with SMTP transport. Env vars: `SMTP_HOST`, `SMTP_PORT`, `SMTP_USER`, `SMTP_PASS`, `SMTP_FROM`. Dev points at [Mailpit](https://github.com/axllent/mailpit) (added to `docker-compose.yml`); prod points at whatever SMTP server is configured.

**Outbox pattern:** every "send email" code path does *not* call SMTP directly. It writes one row to `email_outbox` with `status='pending'` and the rendered subject/body. A simple polling worker drains the outbox.

Why outbox: SMTP is a side-effect on a flaky network. If we send inline and the request fails after sending, we double-send on retry; if SMTP is down, the user-facing action fails. Outbox decouples the action (always succeeds, just queues an email) from the delivery (worker retries with backoff). Adds one table and ~50 lines of worker code; pays for itself the first time SMTP burps.

**Worker:** an `setInterval` loop inside the same Express process. Every 10s, claims up to 10 `pending` rows (`UPDATE ... RETURNING`), sends each via nodemailer, marks `sent` on success or increments `attempts` + sets `last_attempt_at` on failure. After 5 failed attempts, marks `failed`. No separate process needed.

**Templates:** plain TS template functions in `api/src/email/templates/` returning `{ subject, html, text }`. Three templates for v1:
- `invite.ts` — "Your agent <name> invited you to PropStealth." Magic-link button.
- `tenants_shared.ts` — "<agent name> shared <N> tenant <candidate(s)> with you." Lists each tenant (applicant name + property + score). Single deep link or per-tenant links.
- `thread_message.ts` — "<author name> replied about <applicant name>." Excerpt + deep link. Used for both directions (agent→owner replies to a question, owner→agent new question).
- `decision.ts` — "<owner name> <approved|rejected> <applicant name>." Sent to agent only.

**Trigger points** (where code calls `enqueueEmail(...)`):
- `POST /api/agent/invites` (create invite) → `invite`
- `POST /api/agent/tenants/share` (batched share endpoint) → one `tenants_shared` per recipient owner, listing all just-shared tenants for that owner
- `POST /api/tenants/:id/thread` (any message reply) → `thread_message` to the *other* party
- `POST /api/tenants/:id/decision` → `decision` to the agent who created the tenant

**No emails on:**
- `unshare`, `reopen` — these update state in the UI; the next event will email if relevant.
- AI eval completion — agent stays on the tenant page while it runs; we already poll. (If you want one, easy to add.)

**Notification preferences:** v1 has a single owner-side toggle in `/owner/settings`: "Email me when there's tenant activity." Default on. Agents always get notified (the agent sees these events on their dashboard, but emails matter when they're not in the app). Can layer per-event prefs later.

Does the notification design look right? In particular:
1. Outbox + in-process worker (vs. inline send) — OK?
2. The four templates above — anything to add/remove?

lastPrompt: 1. yes, 2. looks good

**API — new files:**

- `api/src/db/migrations/007_create_tenant_tables.sql` — `tenants`, `tenant_documents`, `tenant_evaluations`, `tenant_thread_events`.

**Web — keep as-is:**

input.content:
# PropStealth — Tenant Review MVP

**Date:** 2026-05-03
**Status:** Draft (pending review)
**Supersedes:** `docs/superpowers/archive/2026-04-19-PRD-v0.2.md` and the four older design specs/plans now in `docs/superpowers/archive/`.

---

## 1. Overview

PropStealth's revised v1 scope is a single workflow: **a real estate agent sources prospective tenants for a property owner, uploads supporting documents, lets an AI score them, and shares them with the owner for review.** The owner can ask questions, approve, or reject. The agent can reply to questions and revoke shares.

Everything else from the previous PRD — Inbox Agent, Gmail integration, document vault, portfolio analytics, maintenance, bills — is removed for this milestone and archived. The product is now a focused tenant-review tool with a per-tenant conversation thread.

## 2. Actors

- **Agent** — real estate agent. Signs up directly. Manages many owner-clients.
- **Owner** — property owner / investor. Onboarded via an invite from an agent. Has exactly one agent in v1.

Relationship: 1 agent → many owners. The relationship is created when an agent invites an owner; the owner accepts by clicking a magic link in the invite email and signing in with Google.

## 3. Primary Flow

1. Agent signs in (Google OAuth).
2. Agent invites an owner by email. Invite email contains a single-use, 14-day, magic-link token.
3. Owner clicks the link, signs in with Google, account is created with `role='owner'`, the agent–owner link is established.
4. Agent (or owner) creates one or more **properties** for the owner.
5. Agent creates a **tenant** record for a specific property:
   - Uploads documents, each tagged with a category (Application, ID, Income, Credit, Reference, Other).
   - Clicks "Run AI extraction" → AI returns basic applicant fields, agent reviews and corrects.
   - Clicks "Run Evaluation" → AI returns scores, recommendation, narrative, concerns, verified facts.
6. Agent decides whether to share. If yes:
   - Tenant moves to `shared` status.
   - Owner gets one email per share action (batched if multiple tenants are shared in the same action).
7. Owner clicks the deep link, signs in if needed, and reviews the tenant: documents, AI evaluation, comment thread.
8. Owner can: ask a question (posts to thread → agent gets email), approve, or reject. After approval/rejection the owner can re-open.
9. Agent can: reply in the thread (owner gets email), unshare, re-run evaluation, edit basics.

## 4. Auth & Onboarding

### 4.1 Sign-in

- `/login` shows a role toggle (Owner / Agent) and a single "Continue with Google" button. Email/password fields remain disabled.
- Agents sign up directly via `/login`.
- Owners can also sign in directly via `/login` after their first invite has been accepted.

### 4.2 Invite flow (first-time owner onboarding)

- Agent submits owner's email + name on `/agent/invite`.
- Backend creates a `pending` row in `agent_clients` keyed by that email, generates a 64-char URL-safe `invite_token` with a 14-day expiry, queues the invite email.
- Owner clicks `https://<host>/invite/<token>`.
  - Valid token → "Your agent <name> invited you. Continue with Google."
  - Expired or already-consumed → "This invite link is no longer valid. Ask your agent to resend."
- "Continue with Google" carries the token in the OAuth `state` parameter.
- OAuth callback creates the `users` row with `role='owner'`, marks the token consumed, links the `agent_clients` row to the new user, sets the session cookie, redirects to `/owner` (or to the first shared tenant if any exist).

### 4.3 Subsequent shares

- The agent shares an already-onboarded owner's tenants. Backend enqueues an email containing a deep link `https://<host>/owner/tenants/<id>`.
- If the owner is logged in, the link goes straight to the tenant page.
- If not logged in, Next.js middleware redirects to `/login?next=...`. After Google sign-in they land on the tenant page.

### 4.4 Sessions

- JWT in HTTP-only cookie, 7-day expiry (unchanged from current implementation).
- Middleware on `/agent/*` and `/owner/*` verifies the cookie. Wrong-role access (owner hitting `/agent/*` or vice versa) redirects to the correct dashboard.

## 5. Data Model

Term note: existing column and table names use `owner` (e.g. `properties.owner_id`, `agent_clients`). Keep those terms; do not rename.

### 5.1 Existing tables (kept)

- `users` — `id, email, name, avatar_url, role ('owner'|'agent'), google_id, created_at, updated_at`. No schema change.
- `sessions` — unchanged.
- `agent_clients` — kept; **add** `invite_token VARCHAR(64) UNIQUE`, `invite_token_expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ`, `invite_consumed_at TIMESTAMPTZ`.

### 5.2 `properties` (existing, trimmed)

Drop columns not used in this scope (mortgage holder, insurance, HOA, tax parcel, purchase price, current value).
Final shape: `id, owner_id, created_by_agent_id, address, city, state, zip, property_type, bedrooms, bathrooms, monthly_rent_target, notes, created_at, updated_at`.
Read/write permission: the owner and their linked agent both have full edit access.

### 5.3 `tenants` (new)

| column | type | notes |
|---|---|---|
| `id` | UUID PK | |
| `property_id` | UUID FK → properties | required |
| `created_by_agent_id` | UUID FK → users | |
| `status` | enum | `draft \| evaluating \| ready \| shared \| approved \| rejected` |
| `applicant_name` | TEXT | |
| `email` | TEXT | |
| `phone` | TEXT | |
| `employer` | TEXT | |
| `monthly_income` | NUMERIC | |
| `move_in_date` | DATE | |
| `notes` | TEXT | |
| `shared_at` | TIMESTAMPTZ | nullable |
| `decided_at` | TIMESTAMPTZ | nullable |
| `decision_by_user_id` | UUID FK → users | nullable |
| `decision_note` | TEXT | nullable |
| `created_at` / `updated_at` | TIMESTAMPTZ | |

Status transitions:

```
draft → evaluating → ready → shared → approved | rejected
                       ↑                ↑           ↑
                       └─────── re-run ─┘           │
                                            ←── reopen ─┘
```

Owner can reopen a decided tenant (back to `shared`). Agent can unshare a `shared` tenant (back to `ready`).

### 5.4 `tenant_documents` (new)

`id, tenant_id, category ('application'|'id'|'income'|'credit'|'reference'|'other'), filename, storage_key, mime_type, size_bytes, uploaded_by_user_id, uploaded_at`.

`storage_key` is the path under `api/uploads/` (e.g. `tenants/<tenantId>/<uuid>.pdf`).

### 5.5 `tenant_evaluations` (new)

One row per AI eval run. The latest `complete` row is the "current" eval shown in the UI; older rows are kept for audit.

| column | type |
|---|---|
| `id` | UUID PK |
| `tenant_id` | UUID FK |
| `status` | `running \| complete \| failed` |
| `overall_score` | INT (0–100, nullable) |
| `recommendation` | `low_risk \| review \| high_risk` (nullable) |
| `category_scores` | JSONB — `{ income, credit, history, identity }`, each `{score:int, summary:text}` |
| `summary` | TEXT |
| `concerns` | JSONB — `[{ text, source_document_id }]` |
| `verified_facts` | JSONB — `[{ text, source_document_id }]` |
| `model_used` | TEXT |
| `error` | TEXT (nullable) |
| `created_at` | TIMESTAMPTZ |
| `completed_at` | TIMESTAMPTZ (nullable) |

### 5.6 `tenant_thread_events` (new)

Unified per-tenant timeline.

`id, tenant_id, type ('message'|'shared'|'unshared'|'approved'|'rejected'|'reopened'), author_user_id, body TEXT (only for type='message'), created_at`.

The owner-side and agent-side tenant pages render this list in chronological order. Decision events render as inline cards in the thread, not just as button states elsewhere.

### 5.7 `email_outbox` (new)

`id, to_email, to_user_id (nullable), subject, body_html, body_text, template_key, status ('pending'|'sent'|'failed'), attempts INT DEFAULT 0, last_attempt_at TIMESTAMPTZ, sent_at TIMESTAMPTZ, error TEXT, created_at`.

### 5.8 Tables to drop

Migration 006 drops:
- `agent_runs`, `gmail_connections`, `inbox_emails` (from migration 005)
- `evaluations` (from migration 003) — replaced by `tenants` + `tenant_evaluations`

## 6. Screens

### 6.1 Marketing & auth

- `/` — homepage. Copy pass to match the simpler product.
- `/login` — agent/owner toggle + Google button (existing).
- `/invite/[token]` — invite landing page. Validates token, shows agent name + Continue with Google.

### 6.2 Agent (`/agent/*`)

- `/agent` — Dashboard. List of owner-clients with property and tenant counts by status. "+ Invite Owner" CTA.
- `/agent/owners/[id]` — Owner detail. Properties list (with "+ Add Property") and a flat tenants list filterable by property and status.
- `/agent/owners/[id]/properties/new` and `/agent/owners/[id]/properties/[propId]` — property create/edit.
- `/agent/tenants/new` — wizard: pick owner+property → upload docs → run extraction → confirm basics → run evaluation → review → share or save draft.
- `/agent/tenants/[id]` — tenant detail. Header (name, property, status, decision pill), tabs: Documents / Evaluation / Thread. Action bar: Share / Unshare / Re-run / Edit basics.
- `/agent/invite` — invite owner form (mostly existing).
- `/agent/settings` — name, avatar, sign out.

### 6.3 Owner (`/owner/*`)

- `/owner` — Dashboard. "Tenants to review" cards (status=`shared`), recent thread activity, properties list.
- `/owner/properties` and `/owner/properties/[id]` — list + edit.
- `/owner/tenants` — full tenants list, filterable.
- `/owner/tenants/[id]` — same three-area layout as the agent view, but actions are Approve / Reject / Reopen and the thread has a reply box.
- `/owner/settings` — name, avatar, single notification toggle ("Email me when there's tenant activity"), sign out.

### 6.4 Pages removed

`/owner/inbox`, `/owner/documents`, `/owner/tenant-eval/*`, `/agent/clients/*` (renamed to `/agent/owners/*`), `/agent/pipeline`, `/agent/help-requests`.

## 7. AI Evaluation Pipeline

Two Claude calls via `@anthropic-ai/sdk`, both run inline in the request handler (no job queue for v1). Files are read fresh from disk on each call. Prompt caching is applied to the system prompt and document text since both calls share context.

### 7.1 Step 1 — Extraction

- Triggered when the agent finishes uploading docs and clicks "Run AI extraction" (or automatically on first upload — TBD-by-implementation; default: explicit button).
- Inputs: documents converted to text (PDFs via `pdf-parse`, images sent directly as Claude vision inputs).
- Output: JSON with `applicant_name, email, phone, employer, monthly_income, move_in_date`. Any field the model can't confidently determine is `null`.
- Result pre-populates the tenant form. Agent corrects and saves. The corrected values are the source of truth.

### 7.2 Step 2 — Evaluation

- Triggered when the agent clicks "Run Evaluation."
- Endpoint creates a `tenant_evaluations` row in `status='running'`, returns immediately, then runs the model call in a fire-and-forget async function that updates the row when complete or failed.
- Frontend polls `/api/tenants/:id` every 2s while status is `evaluating`.
- Inputs: confirmed basics, all documents (text and images), property context (rent target, address, beds/baths). Each document is given a stable `document_id` the model is told to cite.
- Output: matches `tenant_evaluations` schema — overall score, recommendation, four category scores (Income, Credit, History, Identity), narrative summary, concerns array, verified facts array. Concerns and verified facts entries cite a `source_document_id`.

### 7.3 Compliance guardrails (system-prompt constants)

- Never reference protected-class attributes (race, religion, national origin, sex, familial status, disability, age, source of income except where legally permitted).
- Every claim cites a source document.
- Output is advisory only; the human owner makes the final decision.
- Defined as a single exported constant in `api/src/agents/tenant-eval/prompts.ts` so it is auditable.

### 7.4 Re-runs and failure

- Each "Run Evaluation" creates a new `tenant_evaluations` row. The "current" evaluation is the latest `complete` row.
- On failure, status is `failed`, `error` populated, UI shows a retry button.

## 8. Notifications & Email

- Provider: `nodemailer` over SMTP. Dev points at Mailpit (added to `docker-compose.yml`); prod points at configured SMTP.
- Outbox pattern: every send writes a `email_outbox` row. A polling worker (`setInterval` inside Express, every 10s, claims up to 10 pending rows via `UPDATE … RETURNING`) sends each via nodemailer. Up to 5 attempts then `failed`.
- Templates (TS functions returning `{subject, html, text}`):
  - `invite` — magic-link invite from agent to new owner.
  - `tenants_shared` — agent shared 1+ tenants. One email per recipient owner per share action; lists every tenant just shared.
  - `thread_message` — bidirectional. Sent to the *other* party when a message is posted on a tenant thread.
  - `decision` — sent to the agent when the owner approves or rejects.
- No emails on `unshare` or `reopen`.
- Owner-side preference toggle in `/owner/settings`: "Email me when there's tenant activity" (default on). Agents always get notified.

## 9. Storage

- Local filesystem under `api/uploads/`, hidden behind a `Storage` interface (`put`, `get`, `delete`) defined in `api/src/storage/local.ts`. Swapping to S3-compatible later is a matter of writing a second implementation.
- Uploads use `multer` with size and MIME-type limits (TBD-by-implementation; sensible defaults: ≤25 MB per file, common doc + image MIME types only).

## 10. Compliance Notes

- Tenant evaluation outputs are advisory. The product UI clearly labels every evaluation as AI-generated.
- The system prompt enforces FCRA + Fair Housing constraints (see §7.3).
- Documents and evaluation outputs are scoped to the owner and their agent. No other user can read them.
- Audit: every state-changing action (share, unshare, decide, reopen, message) creates a row in `tenant_thread_events` with `author_user_id` and timestamp.

## 11. Migration / Code-Change Inventory

### 11.1 Branch

`feat/tenant-review-mvp` off `main`.

### 11.2 API — keep

`index.ts` (re-registers routes), `config.ts` (envs swapped), `db/{client.ts, migrate.ts}`, `middleware/auth.ts`, `routes/auth.ts`, migrations 001/002/004.

### 11.3 API — modify

- `types.ts` — add Tenant, TenantDocument, TenantEvaluation, ThreadEvent, EmailOutbox types; drop inbox/gmail types.
- `routes/properties.ts` — match trimmed schema.
- `routes/clients.ts` — issue `invite_token` and add `GET /api/invites/:token`.

### 11.4 API — delete

`agents/framework.ts`, `agents/inbox/*`, `routes/inbox.ts`, `routes/gmail.ts`, `routes/evaluations.ts`. Migration files 003 and 005 stay on disk (migration history); their tables are dropped by migration 006.

### 11.5 API — new

- Migrations:
  - `006_drop_inbox_tables.sql` — drops `agent_runs, gmail_connections, inbox_emails, evaluations`; adds `invite_token, invite_token_expires_at, invite_consumed_at` to `agent_clients`; trims `properties` columns.
  - `007_create_tenant_tables.sql` — `tenants, tenant_documents, tenant_evaluations, tenant_thread_events`.
  - `008_create_email_outbox.sql`.
- Routes: `routes/tenants.ts`, `routes/tenant-documents.ts`, `routes/invites.ts`.
- Agents: `agents/tenant-eval/{index.ts, extract.ts, evaluate.ts, prompts.ts}`.
- Storage: `storage/local.ts`.
- Email: `email/{transport.ts, outbox.ts, worker.ts, templates/{invite.ts, tenants_shared.ts, thread_message.ts, decision.ts}}`.

### 11.6 Web — keep

`middleware.ts` (small role-gate addition), `app/layout.tsx`, `globals.css`, reusable components (`logo, status-badge, score-badge, stat-card, theme-dot, owner-sidebar, agent-sidebar`), `lib/user-context.tsx`.

### 11.7 Web — modify

- `lib/types.ts` — drop email/inbox types; add tenant types.
- `lib/mock-data.ts` — delete.
- Components: drop `email-card.tsx`. Reuse `client-card`, `attention-card`, `timeline-entry` under their existing or renamed forms where useful.
- `app/(marketing)/page.tsx` — copy pass.
- `app/(dashboard)/owner/layout.tsx` and `agent/layout.tsx` — sidebar updates.
- `app/(dashboard)/owner/page.tsx` and `agent/page.tsx` — rebuilt dashboards.
- `app/(dashboard)/owner/properties/{page.tsx, [id]/page.tsx}` — wire to API; trim fields.
- `app/(dashboard)/owner/settings/page.tsx` — drop Gmail block.
- `app/(dashboard)/agent/invite/page.tsx` — minor updates.

### 11.8 Web — delete

`app/(dashboard)/owner/inbox/`, `owner/documents/`, `owner/tenant-eval/`, `agent/clients/`, `agent/pipeline/`, `agent/help-requests/`.

### 11.9 Web — new

- `app/(auth)/invite/[token]/page.tsx`.
- `app/(dashboard)/owner/tenants/{page.tsx, [id]/page.tsx}`.
- `app/(dashboard)/agent/owners/{page.tsx, [id]/page.tsx, [id]/properties/[propId]/page.tsx, [id]/properties/new/page.tsx}`.
- `app/(dashboard)/agent/tenants/{new/page.tsx, [id]/page.tsx}`.
- Components: `tenant-card.tsx, tenant-thread.tsx, doc-upload.tsx, eval-summary.tsx`.

### 11.10 Root / infra

- `docker-compose.yml` — add Mailpit (`axllent/mailpit`, ports 1025 SMTP / 8025 web UI).
- `api/package.json` — add `nodemailer, @types/nodemailer, multer, @types/multer, pdf-parse`. Drop `googleapis`.
- `api/.env.example` — add `SMTP_HOST, SMTP_PORT, SMTP_USER, SMTP_PASS, SMTP_FROM`. Remove Gmail vars.
- `CLAUDE.md` — overview paragraph rewrite.

## 12. Implementation Phasing

1. Branch + spec write + archive old docs (this commit).
2. Migrations 006/007/008 + removal of old code in one commit.
3. Auth additions: invite tokens + invite landing page.
4. Properties: trim schema + wire pages to API.
5. Tenant CRUD + document upload + local storage.
6. AI extraction + evaluation pipeline.
7. Thread events + share / unshare / decide / reopen actions.
8. Email outbox + worker + templates + Mailpit.
9. Owner and agent dashboards rebuilt.
10. Marketing and login copy pass.
11. Sandbox redeploy + smoke test of the full flow.

## 13. Out of Scope (v1)

- Inbox triage, Gmail integration, any email-reading agent.
- Document vault not tied to a tenant.
- Maintenance, vendor sourcing, bills, utilities.
- Portfolio analytics, cash flow, equity tracking, investor reports.
- Tenant Pipeline (Kanban) and Help Requests pages.
- Email/password auth.
- Native mobile apps.
- Per-event notification preferences (single toggle only).
- Multi-agent owners (an owner has at most one agent).

## 14. Open Items (call out to track during implementation)

- Upload size and MIME-type limits (default ≤25 MB; restrict to PDF + common image types).
- "Run extraction" trigger: explicit button (default) vs auto on first upload.
- Whether to keep the existing static homepage layout or simplify further during the copy pass.
- The exact set of fields in the agent's tenant-creation form beyond what the AI extracts.
